topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a monotonous tune, a soundtrack to my growing unease. I watched Sarah from across the cubicle farm, her movements brisk, her reports always submitted with an almost alarming speed. Today, while reviewing the quarterly budget projections, a number flashed onto my screen that made my breath hitch. It was Sarah’s bonus calculation, and it dwarfed mine by a significant margin. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and deliberately focused on straightening a stray stack of papers. *It’s just a number,* I told myself, my gaze fixed on the texture of the carpet. I decided to wait. I decided to observe the pattern of her work, to let the facts settle before any reaction took hold.

[story 2]

"Did you see the allocation for the new software rollout, Mark?" Liam asked, leaning over my desk, a conspiratorial glint in his eye. He was buzzing with excitement about his recent promotion. I nodded slowly, my hands resting flat on the keyboard. When he mentioned his compensation package, a slight tightening occurred in my chest. I kept my expression neutral, offering a measured, "That sounds substantial," and shifted my attention to the coffee machine. The rhythmic hiss and drip seemed to fill the silence between us. I allowed the comparison to sit there, an unacknowledged weight, letting the situation unfold at its own speed, like watching water seep into dry earth.

[story 3]

I spent the morning meticulously cataloging every interaction with Chloe. She was charming, undeniably so, and her success seemed effortless. When the HR memo detailing the salary bands was circulated, I felt a strange stillness settle over me. I took a slow sip of water, letting the cool liquid trace a path down my throat. I didn't confront her; that felt too volatile. Instead, I started asking more detailed, procedural questions about her projects, focusing entirely on the mechanics of her achievements rather than the outcomes. My voice remained level, almost clinical. I observed the disparity, cataloging the evidence without allowing my internal landscape to become turbulent. It was a slow, deliberate process, like watching geological erosion.
